> I'm not sure I understand your setup, but could you explain why using the "connect to gdbserver" button doesn't work, why gdbserver isn't running on your RTOS, and how gdbserver is connected to the RTOS? TBH I have not used gdbgui in the embedded world, so I may learn something from you :)

These targets are the sort of deeply embedded microcontroller CPU that's too small to run gdbserver on the same chip which is running the program you're interested in debugging. Instead, you connect to that microcontroller over a chip-to-chip debug protocol (usually SWD) through some secondary chip, the debug IC, which usually interacts with a gdbserver process on the host machine via USB.

Looks like this:

```
workstation / debug host running gdbserver <--- USB ---> debug secondary IC <----  chip to chip debug protocol (on ARM, usually SWD) ---> chip with program being debugged
```

The "chip to chip debug protocol" is a real chip-to-chip protocol, i.e. the debug IC directly controls the chip being debugged using a direct hardware link. Think of a headcrab from the Aliens movies: the debug IC, through direct hardware signalling, tells the chip being debugged when to step a single instruction via the SWD protocol, and can read out memory and other state using the same protocol.

All of these details are hidden behind the common gdbserver protocol which is run on the host PC. The gdbserver instance knows how to translate the common network protocol expected by GDB and the USB/etc commands expected by the embedded development environment.

The workstation / debug host is the computer which runs gdbserver. This is the "normal computer" which runs Linux, macOS, Windows, etc.

In this case, I'm using the gdbserver provided by pyOCD (https://github.com/mbedmicro/pyOCD), but there are numerous examples of gdbservers in this area. One important example is https://www.segger.com/products/debug-probes/j-link/tools/j-link-gdb-server/about-j-link-gdb-server/.

There are numerous other gdbserver implementations in this area, though. Beyond J-Link, the Black Magic Debug Probe has one, and there are other examples.

> Also, I would recommend going to Settings in gdbgui and turning on the option Print all sent commands in console, including those sent automatically by gdbgui. This way, you can see exactly what is being sent by gdbgui. You can also run the same commands you had working in command line gdb, but manually in gdbgui and see if it works that way.

I have added a cmake module to the `glad2_cmake` of my fork [here](https://github.com/madebr/glad/tree/glad2_cmake).

Properties:
- able to bootstrap: adding a new `glad_add_library` will correctly regenerate the gl sources on the next build
- only regenerates the gl sources when some argument changes. A clean does not remove the gl sources (although there is an issue)
- the `args.txt` file is needed to be sure that the args for generated files are the same (because the `add_custom_command` comand is removed once the gl sources are generated, we have to manually do some bookkeeping)
- add custom `regenerate` target to give users the ability to force regeneration of the gl sources
- the glad script can be forced by passing `-DGLAD_BIN=$PATH_TO_GLAD` to cmake
- needs the `GLAD_MODULE_PATH` variable defined

Known issue(s):
- When issuing a clean directly after an initial build, without having initial glad sources present, will remove the glad sources. You have to do at least another make before the glad sources will not be removed by the

The library is a wrapper around sk-image and opencv. The advantages are listed at the top of the readme.

One of the use cases I found was to install packages in `postmkvirtualenv`, which I agree could be done with a `pew in`. But another use case was to start a local service on `postactivate`. Since I would expect `pew in` to call `postactivate` prior to executing the command, I don't think it makes sense to call `pew in` from `postactivate`.

In cases like this it would be nice to have the hook execute in a fully activated environment.

That of courses raises problems with the gists on #47 because those would have a dependency on pew - which may not be present in the virtual environment.

Or maybe we don't have a `postactivate` hook.

The error is:
```
AttributeError: type object 'Socket' has no attribute 'shutdown'
```
It's not about blocking/non-blocking, it's that when you were going through the list of methods on `socket` and re-exporting them, this one got missed :-)

I believe that at the syscall level, `shutdown` never blocks, similar to `close`. Probably at the curio level it should do whatever `Socket.close` does.

The `run(coro)` option above describes non-existant behavior.   `run(coro)` always runs the supplied coroutine and non-daemonic tasks to completion. It returns the result of the coroutine.   There are no options to detach from it or have it operate in the background.   If this is wanted, then use `run(spawn(coro, daemon=True))` instead.

I don't anticipate any of those options (a) - (c) being supported.

Will work on docs.   FYI,  there is no relation between the top level `spawn()` function and the `TaskGroup.spawn()` method. Nor is there any kind of hierarchy or relationship between different Task Groups.  For a Task to be part of a group, it must be explicitly spawned from it or added to the group.

One general thought I'll throw out there is that I'm not actually sure if I would describe Curio as an "async framework."   It's definitely a library related to concurrency and it uses async/await syntax for coroutines, but it tends to eschew almost everything else that one typically sees in asynchronous programming  (e.g., callbacks, event loops, Futures, Protocols, etc.).  Needless to say, that makes it a bit of an odd-duck.

In other words, it should be possible to test dbus to determine its viability without triggering warnings.

I imagine it might be possible to capture the stderr, something like:

```
    with capture_stderr():
        try_connect_dbus()
```

I'd feel better with a solution like that. Even better, though, would be for the Python APIs to offer a means to suppress the warnings.

I'm not sure which system is responsible for this message. Would you be willing to track down the software that's producing this warning and get their opinion on how they recommend polling the dbus without triggering the warning?

Failing that, let's just suppress the output.

So you would not want to specifically want to block upcoming versions? E.g. ">= 1.0" is not good?

Usually when looking at python *libraries* (not *applications*), the general approach seems to be that minimum version is specified but the maximum version has been left open on purpose. This allows people to use the library with newer versions, although naturally you can never know for sure whether it works "out-of-the-box".

For example, [flask](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/master/setup.py) 
````
install_requires=[
    'Werkzeug>=0.7',
    'Jinja2>=2.4',
    'itsdangerous>=0.21',
    'click>=2.0',
],
````
and [requests](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/setup.py)

````
extras_require={
    'security': ['pyOpenSSL>=0.14', 'cryptography>=1.3.4', 'idna>=2.0.0'],
    'socks': ['PySocks>=1.5.6, !=1.5.7'],
},
````

declare their dependencies this way.

From library user perspective I think it would be nice to allow usage with newer versions of dependencies, but perhaps documenting "compatible versions" in the library homepage, for example.

```
[defaults]
transport=ssh

[ssh_connection]
ssh_args = -o ForwardAgent=yes -o ControlMaster=auto -o ControlPersist=60s -o ControlPath=~/.ssh/ansible-ssh-%h-%p-%r
pipelining=True
```

An other way to run tests faster is to use parallel execution using pytest-xdist: https://testinfra.readthedocs.org/en/latest/invocation.html#parallel-execution

### Why Not Dilation

Three reasons:

### Tests

Updated test to:

```python
def test_mask_contour():
    # test mask contour is created
    mask = np.zeros((234, 456), dtype=np.int)
    mask[100:150, 300:400] = 255

    sm = WordCloud(mask=mask, contour_width=1, contour_color='blue')
    sm.generate(THIS)
    sm_array = np.array(sm)
    sm_total = sm_array[100:150, 300:400].sum()

    lg = WordCloud(mask=mask, contour_width=20, contour_color='blue')
    lg.generate(THIS)
    lg_array = np.array(lg)
    lg_total = lg_array[100:150, 300:400].sum()

    sc = WordCloud(mask=mask, contour_width=1, scale=2, contour_color='blue')
    sc.generate(THIS)
    sc_array = np.array(sc)
    sc_total = sc_array[100:150, 300:400].sum()

    # test `contour_width`
    assert_greater(lg_total, sm_total)

    # test contour varies with `scale`
    assert_greater(sc_total, sm_total)

    # test `contour_color`
    assert_equal(sm_array[100, 300], np.array([0, 0, 255]))
```

These tests rely on the fact that contour is drawn inside and outside edge. These examples show the images produced by the test with a red box around the edge for different values of `contour_width`. I assert that sum of blue pixels within red rectangle increase with `contour_width` and `scale`. Lastly, `contour_color` is tested by checking that a pixel within the contour is actually blue.

Weird, but I'm glad it's working. One thing I noticed in the code sample you provided: 

You're checking for `replylimit.remaining` once per iteration of the `while` loop and then replying to all mentions found in the outer loop within a subloop. What's probably happening is that you have, say, 1 reply remaining when you check for mentions in the outer loop. Then you get back, say, 5 mentions in the `api.GetMentions()` call. You then enter the for loop attempting to reply to the 5 new mentions, but you've only got one allowed reply left, resulting in the rate limit error when you try to reply to the second mention (i.e., the 2nd iteration of the for loop)

You could fix this by checking the reply limit within the for loop, but for your use case, I'd suggest using the streaming endpoints. I wrote up a post a little while ago on using them to track a hashtag, but it's basically the same principle for replying to tweets:

https://iseverythingstilltheworst.com/blog/2016/05/28/capturing_twitter_streams/

The MD5 Hash check is just for the Miniconda installer and its existence is for some very specific conditions:
* Ensures that someone has not hijacked the download link and somehow let you snag a miniconda which may report your Travis Secure envs (has not happened, but the check is basically a free action, so I've always just left it in. A bunch of copy-paste between projects, later it has just propagated)
* Prevent getting the wrong Miniconda version. Sometimes the Miniconda folks will update their download links for `latest` *before* updating the MD5 hashes, this can lead to some very unexpected behavior, especially on major Conda version upgrades, so this also traps that. (This has happened more times than I thought it should)
* Some of the projects I have worked on pinned to a maximal version of Conda, so we used that to make sure we did not upgrade due to bad links. Much less common now.

The caching is an interesting idea, but I don't think caching the conda install directory is worth it. As I understand how the Travis Caches work: Download directory, {do Travis things}, If directory has changed at all: re-cache.

Under that model, the directory would almost always re-cache again because of how frequently conda packages update. It may save some boot up time, but will almost always result in pulling down and re-caching, which can chew up time at the end if you are pushing the maximum.

Another reason you may not want to do that is testing philosophy. In the models I follow, you want your tests to replicate the use case of the most basic user of your code as possible. If that user meets the conditions of "Person who knows bare minimal of what your code does and the bare minimal of the supporting software ecosystem, and just wants to pick it up and use it," then you want to replicate the conditions of a user pulling down fresh the bare minimum to get up and going. That is just one style though and if practical Travis limits are becoming an issue, then maybe the Cache is the right option.

Just my opinion though.

Oh, and don't use the Caching system if you are using the Travis Xcode 7.3 image, its bugged in that version and they will not fix it due to it be deprecated.

> After setting up KVSession I have session ids handed out to user's session when the come to the site. when they log in, their session is now authorized to see protected views. When the user logs out they are no longer able to see the protected views but the session is maintained. I can log back in and the session is still the same.

Flask-Login, Flask-KVSession and your app use slightly different approaches here, you just need to reconcile them.

You are storing authorization ("is allowed to see X") and authentication ("logged in as U") in the same session. As a result, the same information ("X can see U") is found in two different places:

1. In your database (I assume it comes from there) there's some sort of record of "allow U to see X"
2. In the session there is now "the owner of this session is allowed to see X".

If you change one or the other, these go out of sync. This is bad. If the administrator revoked U's priviledges to see X, they change would not be effective as long as U is able to hold onto his session.

It works the other way around as well (session gets altered), but that case is not very likely.

A solution to your problem is simply not storing any authorization-information in the session. 9 out of 10 cases you do only want to store one thing inside the session: The username. When a user requests a view that is access restricted, check every time against the database (or whereever the definite location for this piece of information is), not the session.

There are some use cases where you would want to keep extra information inside a session. An example would be a shopping cart - you can fill it up without being logged in and it carries over to your new account. But these are specific cases.

> The edge case I'm looking at is if I steal that session id, I can gain access to another machine so long as they are logged in. With the session id being maintained, a malicious user who has that session may gain access if the real user logs back in. The behavior that I want is that if the session id is captured and the user is logged out, that the session id can never be used to log back in again. Once the user logs out that id is never given access again.

You've almost identified the initial sole reason for flask-kvession's existance =). What you're describing is simply stealing a cookie and it is a widely known problem. The  issue with Flask's original client-based sessions is that they cannot be invalidated easily - it's the price you pay for the incredible light weight.

The "horror" scenario is: User gets his cookie stolen, now the attacker can do everything the user can do. When using client-side sessions (like Flask does), even if the user logs out, only his cookie is lost, the attacker can still do whatever he wants.

With flask-kvsession, you can actually destroy the session, which you should do upon logout. That removes the session on the server side, it will not be found again and the attacker is logged out.

There is also the danger of session fixation. If the user later on (or initially) logs in and the attacker has already stolen the session id, the attacker's session will get "upgraded" as well. An example:

* User visits site, his session is now 'ABC'
* Attacker steals cookie, now knows id 'ABC'
* some times passes
* User visits site again, still has session 'ABC', and logs in
* Session 'ABC' is now "logged in" and available to the attacker and the user

To rectify this, the session id should be regenerated (see below).

> My workaround is to completely delete the session upon logout. Ideally I'm thinking I would like to have a non-repeating unique id for the logged in sessions. Almost like having two sessions.I'm not seeing any discussion of this out on the internet so I'm curious if I'm implementing this wrong somehow.

Your "workaround" is the correct way to use it. You only need to think about these things if you have data other than the username that you need to persist. Normally there should not be any.

Here's how the process is supposed to go (no Flask-Login here at this point):

1. User visits site, gets a session ID if needs one (usually does not need one, since you're not storing anything but the username in it).
2. User logs in. You do not know if there has been a session already or not, but it is simple to ``regenerate`` is. This will ensure a new session id is created and sent to the client, containing any possibly old session data. Note that if a cookie is stolen here, there is simply nothing you can do.
3. After a while, the user logs out. ``destroy`` the session to make sure potentially stolen sessions are invalid as well.

Here's how it still works if everything goes wrong:

4. User's browser is buggy and keeps the stale session cookie around. The session is empty (destroyed) on the server side, so if he presents with it, the server can either generate a new one (new session id), which is fine or create a blank session with the old id (bad idea) or just throw an exception (secure, but not user friendly. This used to be a bug, which I hope is fixed now =).

Security wise, generating an empty session is the worst case, so we'll go with that:

5. The attacker somehow knows the users session ID and now the user logs in with his old session id. But since ``regenerate`` is called after login, only the user gets the new session ID.

I hope that clears things up.

* I am not a fan of flask-login -- but it is *not* to blame at this point. Just make sure you ``destroy()`` after logout and ``regenerate()`` after login.
* If you need some sort of access control, you can look at another project of mine for some ideas. It's a bit complex and could use a little polish, but it is what I use when I am messing around with these things: https://pythonhosted.org/alcohol/ It could really use some more feedback on real world usage. =)

And the actual bug here is: ``destroy()`` and ``regenerate()`` are not documented. They are in the source, but don't show up on the official documentation sites. So, until I fix that, here's

* A link to the docs, from the source: https://github.com/mbr/flask-kvsession/blob/master/flask_kvsession/__init__.py#L89
* An example on how to use it:

```python

from flask import session

# ...

# after login succeeded:
session.regenerate()
session['username'] = ...
```

TODO:
* Fix docs
* Write tests for these specific use cases
* Give some advice (in docs) on how to handle this common use case.

Hi @hildogjr 

Bug reports should be complete working examples rather than snippets of code to allow the issue to be reproduced.

However, even without a working example there are a few issues here:

1. Excel formulas and functions should be uppercase.
2. Formulas should use commas not semi-colons (there is a mix of both in the above example).

These issues and others are explained in the [Working with Formulas](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_formulas.html) section of the docs and in particular [Dealing with formula errors](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_formulas.html#dealing-with-formula-errors).

So the correct formula probably should be something like this:

```
=IFERROR(AND(IF(K7="",G7,K7)<100000,M7>LOOKUP(IF(K7="",G7,K7),{1,10,50},{1.3,6.8,16.9})),"")
```

However, you also need to check if Excel supports this type of formula for conditional formatting.

Hm, that does sound painful. You have an interesting use case -- being able to retrieve requirements from endpoints. Currently there's nothing that handles that, however I'd be open to considering it as it seems very useful.

My first thoughts on this matter:

Re-exposing the requirements as an attribute on the endpoint, so you could do something like:

```python
func = app.view_functions[endpoint]
func.__requirements__  # (is_admin, Has('hello'))
```

This exposing is straight forward for endpoints that are functions and CBV that have their entire access governed by a single requires. There are two complications that come to mind, the first is very hard to deal with:

```python
@app.route('/')
@some_other_deco
@requires(...)
def some_handler():
    pass
```

`requires` and `app.route` are well behaved decorators, but is `some_other_deco` well behaved -- does it use `functool.wraps` -- even if it's on Py2.7 it might not matter because there was a bug with `wraps` that wasn't fixed until 3.3 that would result in clobbering. So that's an issue. The only way to be 100% to avoid it is to place requires second to last, only after `app.route` so this discipline falls to the end user.

The other complication is more subtle but actually easier to deal with from a library point of view:

```python3
class SomeView(MethodView):
    decorators = [requires(...)]

    @requires(...)
    def get(self):
        pass
```

There are now two sets of requirements that are logically AND'd together, however since the endpoint class is exposed by the `as_view` constructor, a helper method can be made to retrieve and assemble this into a coherent set of requirements.

The final issue that I don't think is able to be handled as Allows is right now is when a Requirement internally switches its actions based on the request's HTTP action. However, if that's being done its probably troublesome in other ways as well and to be frank, I don't think multiplexing is a good move with either requirements or endpoints (as a consequence, I'm a big supporter of CBVs).

Anyways, those are fiddly points that would need to be addressed. As for gathering the requirements and checking them, I think something along these lines would be up to the task:

```python
def requirements_for_endpoint(endpoint):
    handler = current_app.view_functions[endpoint]
    requirements = getattr(handler, '__requirements__`, ())
    return requirements
```

And running them might look like:

```python
def allowed_endpoint(endpoint):
    return bool(Permission(*requirements_for_endpoint(endpoint)))
```

However you'd only ever be able to use in situations where there is an active application context, but I think that's a small price to pay.

I'd say this is a pretty important issue for anybody who require graceful shutdown of processes.  Relying on __del__ is a horrible practice that should be avoided if possible, and IMHO if necessary
even warrants a backward incompatible change.

Doing `del(self._db)` may work for that specific application, but in general you'd have to carefully
del() any other references to the object as well, and who knows what happens if there are circular references.

NOTE: Using a weakref callback you could possibly set a flag to ensure the database was closed
(well, implicitly by side effect of being garbage collected, trying to be an optimist here:)
pseudocode:


```py
class RocksDBWrapper:
    _db_collected = False

    def open(self):
        self._db = rocksdb.DB(...)
        self._dbref = weakref.ref(self._db, self._on_db_collected)
       
    def _on_db_collected(self, ref):
        self._db_collected = True

    def close(self):
        while not self._db_collected:
            try:
                del(self.db)
            except AttributeError:
                pass
            gc.collect()
```

I think this is a pretty serious issue that needs to be resolved. Hopefully I, or someone else get some time to look into it.

Finally tracked this one down.  Way back in https://github.com/rdegges/django-twilio/commit/53693789575b1786afca52fe1b15047e8e07207d, instead of doing the proper thing and creating a second migration, the initial migration was updated.  The old version didn't contain the `Credential` model, so if you had already run the initial migration before this change, you never created the `django_twilio_credential` table in your database.

Here's what you can do to fix it:
- create an empty table in your database named `django_twilio_credential` (so that the next step doesn't fail)
- `manage.py migrate django_twilio zero`
- `manage.py migrate django_twilio`

@lastboy 

There is no way to retrieve [those limits](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/limits.html#ec2) via the API; AWS provides information on some limits via the API and others via Trusted Advisor (see http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/limits.html for details), but the instance-type-specific Running Instances limits aren't among them.

Until AWS fixes this, you must specifically pass in the new limit values to AWSLimitChecker, either via the ``-L/--limit`` command line flag or (if using via Python) via [set_limit_override()](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/awslimitchecker.checker.html#awslimitchecker.checker.AwsLimitChecker.set_limit_override) or [set_limit_overrides()](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/awslimitchecker.checker.html#awslimitchecker.checker.AwsLimitChecker.set_limit_overrides).

e.g. at my current employer, we rely on [Trusted Advisor](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/limits.html#trusted-advisor-data) and [API](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/limits.html#limits-retrieved-from-service-apis) to populate the limit values they can, but maintain JSON files for each region for each of our accounts that we manually update with the limits that Trusted Advisor and the APIs don't provide information on.

I know this is pretty ugly, but there's no way to retrieve the limits programmatically via the APIs, and screen-scraping the Console is both slow and explicitly forbidden in the terms of service.

So it looks like you have 7 neopixels per digit whereas the `sevensegment` class expects 8 LEDs per digit. 

Something like the below code might work (untested, though)

```python
import time
from luma.led_matrix.device import ws2812
from luma.core.virtual import sevensegment

_DIGITS = {
  # Think this corresponds to the bit positions 
  ' ': 0b00000000,
  '0': 0b11101110,
  '1': 0b00100010,
  '2': 0b11010110,
  # etc
}

def neopixel_sevenseg_mapper(text, not_found="_"):
    try:
        undefined = _DIGITS[notfound]
        iterator = iter(text)
        while True:
            char = next(iterator)
            yield _DIGITS.get(char, undefined)
    except StopIteration:
        pass

device = ws2812(width=6, height=7)
sevenseg = sevensegment(device, segment_mapper=neopixel_sevenseg_mapper)

count = 0
while True:
    sevenseg.text = "{0:06d}".format(count);
    count += 1
    time.sleep(1)
```

I have reworked the PR, I forgot to test some cases and it wasn't working as expected.

POSIX expansion is working as in most dotenv libraries but it is not truly POSIX compilant.

`${FOO}` and `FOO_${BAR}` will be replaced without problems, but supporting default values in the form of `${FOO:-default_value}` will not.

Truly posix expansion can be achieved using https://github.com/kojiromike/parameter_expansion but I didn't want to include external libraries without asking you first. For me the current behavior is good enough.

The idea of a unity build is that it comes as one .c file that the user of the code includes as such. This enables the compiler to inline all the code, for greater performance. So there is no point on the CRoaring side to compiling the unity build except maybe for testing purposes.

The approach we follow with the amalgamation script is identical to sqlite's approach. In turn, sqlite's unity builds are found just about everywhere. It is a sound, well-tested and robust practice.

We have dynamic libraries largely to save storage and memory but these points are not obviously beneficial here since CRoaring is tiny compared to today's machines...

Another point is that making your library as dependency-free as possible, and as easy to install as possible... makes it more useful...

I agree and understand that it is impractical to make this behavior the default. Another possibility would be to offer an option (not the default) for not truncating the search, and reporting the entire graph of dependencies, and their status with respect to Python 3. The description of that option, and possibly a message in the output produced could explicitly declare that:

1. the result does *not* imply that Python 3 cannot be used,
2. no packages that don't declare Python 3 support are reachable directly (i.e., reachable only via packages that declare support), if that has been found to be the case,
3. inconsistencies should not be reported to this project when running in exhaustive mode.

Of course, it still has the potential of triggering a lot of unintended load here.

For premature declaration of Python 3 support by the top package (only---the one that we name as argument), it is simply something that one should not do ("we are all consenting adults here"). I have in mind the enthusiastic developer though, who started porting their project to Python 3, then midway discovered that `caniusepython3` exists, and calls it, but has already modified the Trove classifiers in their package's `setup.py` (because they did so to not forget later). This refers only to the project being checked, not to other projects. It is more about the definition of `caniusepython3`:

- whether it checks all dependencies, vs
- whether it checks the given package (treating itself as a dependency), and then all dependencies.

In other words, it is unclear whether the graph's root is included or not in the definition of what `caniusepython3` does. My understanding was that it checks dependencies, so I was a little surprised to find out that what the top package does matters at all. So, it could be a matter of definition whether to change this behavior or not (simple is better, so uniformly treating the entire graph likely allows for a simpler implementation). It would be helpful though to emphasize somewhere (e.g., in the README) that the top package is included too, and perhaps also at runtime that the search truncated at the top package (which would alarm a developer about prematurely editing the Trove classifiers in their `setup.py`, while preparing for Python 3).

Having said this, one should checkout an earlier commit, and check that, but I would expect this to rarely happen the first time one uses `caniusepython3`.

Because `self.qrcode_file` doesn't take a PIL `Image` instance, it expects a `django.core.files.File` instance.
```python
class Ticket(models.Model):
    booked_at = models.DateTimeField(default=timezone.now)
    qrcode_file = models.ImageField(upload_to='qrcode', blank=True, null=True)
    bought = models.BooleanField(default=False)

    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.booked_at = timezone.now()
        if self.bought:
            filename = 'qr.png'.format(self.booked_at.strftime('%Y-%m-%d'))
            qr_file = ContentFile('', name=filename)
            qrcode.make('some data').save(qr_file, format='PNG')
            self.qrcode_file = qr_file
        super(Ticket, self).save(*args, **kwargs)
```

I've seen a similar issue before - during distributed training, each machine might get a different number of batches because MXNet prefetches data from the dataset. That means very likely for the last epoch, the one machine has a fewer number of batches than the other, and since it's dist-sync mode, the machine with more batches will wait for the other machine indefinitely. A temporary walk-around is to train n + 1 epochs and explicit break at the n-th epoch in the code..

@gamebusterz There are some usecases where priority is useful:

You have a workflow of tasks containing different families of tasks:
- A type of Task used to compute data used in your webapp (Example: playlist prefered by users)
- A type of Task to report user actions on your webapp (Example: data used to train machine learning models)
- A type of Task to compute some informations about your business

The three family of tasks are unrelated (directly and indirectly).

But the first task family has higher priority (because it's used directly in your webapp by real users).

You workflow looks like:

````python

class MyBusinessWorkflow(luigi.Task):

    requires(self):
         yield ComputeUsersActions() # priority 60
         yield ComputeUsersPlaylists() # priority 100
         yield ComputeBusinessReport() # priority 20
````

It means luigi will execute firstly the highest priority. But if you have multiple workers, luigi will not wait highest priority task is done.

Priority is just used to organise unrelated tasks execution, not to give a precise order.

@parisni The basic usage over HTTPS is
```
from sqlalchemy.engine import create_engine
engine = create_engine(
        'presto://<user>:<password>@<host>:<port>/hive',
        connect_args={'protocol': 'https'})
```
This will automatically use HttpBasicAuth. Note that you must include the password in your connection string. Also this will automatically use SSL. requests package relies on certifi package. Keep those libs up to date.

If you need to use your own SSL Cert Verification, pass in the path to the cert like this
```
from sqlalchemy.engine import create_engine
engine = create_engine(
        'presto://<user>:<password>@<host>:<port>/hive',
        connect_args={'protocol': 'https', 
                                  'requests_kwargs': {'verify': '/path/to/certfile'}
 }
)
```
For Client Side Certificates
```
from sqlalchemy.engine import create_engine
engine = create_engine(
        'presto://<user>:<password>@<host>:<port>/hive',
        connect_args={'protocol': 'https', 
                                  'requests_kwargs': {'cert': ('/path/client.cert', '/path/client.key')}
 }
)
```
reference: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/

it turns out the error message is innocuous.  it is caused by the following line in distribute.sh:
```
root_directory=$(basename $(try tar tjf $pfilename|head -n1))
```

Could you confirm that you provided a working write key and an incorrect master key? In the case that an incorrect master key is provided as well as a correct write key, Keen Client shouldn’t even notice that the master key is incorrect, because the more restricted write key should be used first according to the principle of least privilege ([for more on PoLP, Wikipedia says it better than I know how](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_privilege)).

From scanning the code, it doesn’t look like the Python SDK for Keen uses the master key at all. One could argue that we should use the master key in the case that (a) the user doesn’t provide a write key, or (b) the user provides a write key that doesn’t work, but I think that would be a separate discussion.

Hi Grisha,

In fact the idea behind this chart (and the whole airbnb medium article) is the opposite. They wanted to point out that the pvalue can fluctuate through time, go below the signifiance threshold, and then stay there forever or not. 

The chart show this : if you stop the experiment represented here around day 10, you commit type 1 error. But I you let the experiment run for a few more days, you see that the pvalue in fact "converges" around its true value. 

To recap, this **does not** provide an early stopping criteria. This helps to monitor wether the pvalue has still an erratic behaviour (so we can't stop the experiment at this moment), or if it hasn't changed sinced a "long time" (to be defined). For me the ideal criteria is : 
- look at the true statistical early stopping criteria (the aim of this package)
- accept this results iff the pvalue graph has converged

Expanding on my last comment on an improvement of the current gridding routine.  Currently the gridding algorithm in Py-ART loops over all points in the grid, finds radar gates within a given distance by searching a k-d or Ball tree for each grid point and then performing a Cressman or Barnes weighting to interpolate the values of the grid points.  This is computationally expensive are requires at least N log(M) operations (N : number of grid points, M : number of gates) for finding gates and significantly more time if multiple gates fall into the search distance.  A more efficient algorithm would loop over the radar gates, determining which grid points they should contribute to and performing the weighted interpolation using an online algorithm.  This algorithm would have a linear time complexity in M which should have better performance that the existing method.

Hey @McFoggy ,

> in fr language, shouldn't "31,2" be interpreted as a single real number (trente et un virgule deux in french or thirty one dot 2 in english) instead of 2 distinct integers?

It is actually the case, `31,2` is correctly interpreted as a decimal number, but what happens under the hood is that there is a data augmentation step in `snips-nlu` which creates many small variations of the dataset utterances in order to improve robustness. One of the variations affects the punctuation, and in your case generates a new sample with "31 2" instead of "31,2".

I hope this is a bit clearer now 😉

I think that what Ines refers to as a reconstruction but not a model, as something that is not initialized with constraints (i.e. environmental conditions or context-specific constraints), is related to my comment that a model without enviromental constraints (I still call it a model) is still something that is valid to be published.

Nonetheless, I think these un-initialised models (or reconstructions) should still be subject to validation and should pass most (if not all) of memote tests, after being initialized with the most possibly relaxed set of constraints (i.e. simulation of growth on complete media).

OSX provided pythons are semi-notorious for having issues that hit developers.

If you don't mind, and don't have brew, setup instructions are here: http://brew.sh
Basically:
```console
$ ruby -e "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/master/install)"
```

And once you have brew, just:
```console
$ brew install python
```

Check to see that the brew python2.7 is 1st on your PATH:
```console
$ hash -r
$ which python2.7
```

The brew python will be under /usr/local/ if you did a vanilla brew setup.
If which instead hands back a /usr/bin python2.7 (ie: the OSX one), then you'll need to update your PATH to ensure the brew python2.7 "wins".

I've just tested it using the line below (with redactions), and was able to access a stream in another account:

```
AWSCredentialsProvider = STSAssumeRoleSessionCredentialsProvider|arn:aws:iam::<account id>:role/pfifer-cross-account|pfifer-session
```

I've tested this with version 1.11.151, and 1.11.129 of the STS SDK.

To get more information [I've updated the pom.xml](https://gist.github.com/pfifer/d1e884cf83ff9b22d1e479c6a5cc4b72) to include some logging libraries.  You can use this without a logging configuration file, but it will default to DEBUG.  You can configure the logging using these steps:
1. In you application directory create a directory called logback (you can call it something else if you want to).
2. Download [this logback.xml file](https://gist.github.com/pfifer/51ed12978d3cde5dc9c79340d8d8ca35), and save it to logback/logback.xml
3. Start the script again like: ```CLASSPATH_PREFIX=./logback ./bin/multilang-daemon config/kcl.local.properties```

Hi @DPH 

It is great that you want to test. I can see that I wasn't very clear in my example code.

These data structures are meant to be exactly for the music services what the Didl data structure are for the music library. So you should ever need to instantiate them yourself, but you will get the returned to you, when you search or browse a music service. I order to test it, you need a functioning music (right now quite a few of them have authentication issues), but if you do have a functioning one, you can test. I'm using the "YouSee Musik" music service in the examples, but you will then need to substitute that with your working music service. First to show the searching:

```python
>>> from soco.music_services.music_service import MusicService
>>> from soco.discovery import any_soco
>>> 
>>> ym = MusicService('YouSee Musik')
>>> albums = ym.search('albums', 'the last resort')
>>> print(len(albums))
20
>>> print(albums)
SearchResult(items=[<soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d6f2b0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d6fd30>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d6fe80>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d6f7f0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d6f588>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d6f5c0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80cf9be0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80cf9cf8>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80cf9f60>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80e0ec18>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d022e8>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d023c8>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d02ba8>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d02da0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d0af98>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d16128>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d16320>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d16d30>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d16e80>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSAlbum object at 0x7f3c80d16fd0>], search_type='albums')
>>> for album in albums:
...  print(album, ";", album.title)
... 
<MSAlbum title="The Last Resort"> ; The Last Resort
<MSAlbum title="In the Last Resort"> ; In the Last Resort
<MSAlbum title="The Last Resort"> ; The Last Resort

<MSAlbum title="Last Resort (Karaoke Version) [in the Style of Papa Roach]"> ; Last Resort (Karaoke Version) [in the Style of Papa Roach]

>>> zone.add_to_queue(album)
```

So, searching returns a SearchResult object (exactly like search the music library), which is a sequence of the search result object (it can be indexed and iterated over like a list). The object that it consist of, as you can see are MSAlbum object. They present themselves as such (which is one reason for making them) and they know to do be added to the queue.

We can also use the music service to browse an already returned object to get its children in the large content tree that a music service seems to have: 

```python
>>> print(to_play)
<MSAlbum title="The Last Resort">
>>> tracks = ym.get_metadata(to_play)
>>> tracks
SearchResult(items=[<soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d04390>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d04a20>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d04828>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d04470>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d04160>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d02908>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d02f28>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d16748>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d6f4e0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c810957f0>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80cf9a90>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d0ab00>, <soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d0d198>], search_type='browse')
>>> tracks[0]
<soco.music_services.data_structures.MSTrack object at 0x7f3c80d04390>
>>> track = tracks[0]
>>> zone.add_to_queue(track)
```

Of course if you search to something like a genre, you can browse it in many more levels: genre -> artist -> albums -> tracks etc.

I hope that makes it a little clearer ... and that it works for you.

Regards Kenneth

So, the problem here is when `md5-password` is configured that there is actually another command called `md5-base64`.  In the case of `md5-base64` being unset, `MD5()` will attempt take your string and append equal signs until it base64 decodes.  I believe that behavior violates the principle of least astonishment and should be removed.  @thomas-mangin do you agree?  If so, what should the default behavior of `md5-base64` be - `True` or `False`?

As a stopgap for both of your issues above, if you set `md5-password` to the  plaintext password and `md5-base64` to `False`, then your configurations will work until this is fixed.

@hozn regarding the interpolation, you're partially right, but there's also the fact that autobahn-python supports Twisted and asyncio (and their different default logging systems) from one codebase. We're doing logging more-like Twisted's new logger, which allows delaying the string interpolation as long as possible so that e.g. if you're not printing ``.debug()`` messages, then you don't pay the price to format any of those strings. So, no, we won't do ``.format()`` ahead of time. But, the ``aio`` flavour of txaio does need to hook into the default logging properly so it works with your use-case.

Perhaps the image is considered "dangling" as per https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/builder/rootfs/etc/confd/templates/check-repos#L16, which would be considered a Docker bug. `check-repos` literally checks and removes images that have the same matching name as the repositories in /home/git as well as dangling images, so that's the only way I could think that it's being removed.

From docker's docs on `dangling=true`:

> This will display untagged images, that are the leaves of the images tree (not intermediary layers). These images occur when a new build of an image takes the repo:tag away from the image ID, leaving it untagged. A warning will be issued if trying to remove an image when a container is presently using it. By having this flag it allows for batch cleanup.

- I think it would involve polling the transaction pool, but that carries the risk of missing transactions.
- Infura does not yet have any tx pool apis.
- API is node dependent (not an impossible problem).

Maybe we could add a recipe to the docs for an alternate way from node filters to get information on pending transactions, in lieu of having the filtering middleware handle it.

What do you mean exactly with "getting access to my source to make changes"? You can edit your vhdl source files in Sigasi after you open your project.

If you add more files you have to re-generate the project. The point of generating a sigasi project in the first place is that the project itself is not the original source of information of what files and libraries you have. In this use case VUnit defines what source files and libraries you have and the sigasi project is generated from that. This is a specific instance of a general principle that says that any time the original source of information is changed all output artifacts that was generated from that original source of information must be re-generated.

This is just one way of doing it that I myself prefer. One could also imagine the other way around where VUnit would use a sigasi project as the original source of information on what files and libraries there is. Then the sigasi project would be checked into version control.

The reason I prefer my way of doing it is that I like the files and libraries to be defined just by the way they are physically located in the folder structure without me having to individually name each file in some list in a project.

As a side note the visual representation in a sigasi project generated in this example is "virtual" in the sense that it is organized around the library names rather than the physical folder locations of the files. If you want it to be organized around the folder location instead you can change the argument to the `add_link` method to be the relative path of the file compared to the project root.

It definitely looks like this is a bug, which I came across today... 

"az vm create" will create a subnet in the wrong resource group if you don't specify an absolute path upfront and the subnet is in a different subscription. 

When you attempt to change the subnet (to put in the correct address range) you can't do any of the following:

- Remove a NIC / IP config (must always have at least one)
- Add a second NIC / IP config in a different resource group (even after stop-deallocate of the VM)
- Set the NIC subnet directly (to force it to the correct one)

So basically you are forced to destroy and recreate the VM from scratch.

Hi @bewatts, I definitely understand your pain in configuring application gateway. 

> Is it actually not finished until after 20 minutes, or is the API being extra conservative about reporting something in 'Updating' vs 'Succeeded' state?

From my experience, there are a number of things you can do while the gateway is in the "updating" state. Things like creating ports, settings etc. I have noticed that if you are going to upload an SSL cert, you should wait until the gateway is ready, and then after the cert is uploaded, wait until it is ready again. 

The application-gateway create commands should all have the --no-wait parameter. This will return you to the command line instead of waiting for the gateway to update. Try doing your configuration steps using --no-wait and then issuing a wait command, like so:

```
az network application-gateway create .... --no-wait
az network application-gateway frontend-port create .... --no-wait
az network application-gateway wait ... --updated
```
This should reduce your time considerably. 

Another alternative would be to do an ARM template deployment for the application gateway. You can do that through the `az group deployment create` command.

If you specify a subnet name, then the subnet must be in the same subscription you are logged in to and it must be in the same resource group your VM will be created in.  If that's not the case, then the CLI will create the subnet in that location. If you want to use an existing subnet in a different subscription or resource group, you must specify the full ARM ID.

What would say is the bug here?  That the CLI creates the subnet if it isn't found is intentional. What errors do you get when attempting to change the subnet?

There are some scenarios where the only way to "fix" an erroneous VM deployment is to destroy it and recreate it (for example, if you wanted a multi-NIC VM but created it with only one inadvertently).

You would probably want to keep it in case someone started the process via the rest API and wanted to finish it via the CLI.  You can add text to help to clarify this if you want. In either case, the question has been answered so I'll close this issue.

This appears to be a year old bug associated with newer versions of ``setuptools`` (see https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/951). The forced reinstall of ``six`` occurs while ``pip`` is attempting to query package metadata; because ``six`` is temporarily missing, this triggers a fault. Given that this occurs sporadically, it does appear to be a true race condition between setup and pip.

The discussion thread above suggests pinning ``setuptools>=36.0.0`` may solve this problem, as that version avoids the race condition by coming with its own internal version of ``six``. I am unsure what the implications are if another, external version of ``six`` is installed or used alongside ``setuptools``.

There are edge cases though.

Here is an (edge-case) example of how messages can be lost if a job has an unfortunate downtime (however short):
If you are not committing offsets then what may happen is:
- You reach EOF
- Your job sits there waiting for more messages for a long time
- Offsets expire
- Job is still running fine without being able to commit offsets
- Job crashes, got shut down for re-deployment, etc.
- New messages are pushed to the topic
- Job wakes up. 

Because offsets were expired despite the job was running and attempting to re-commit its offsets previously, this job will lose messages. The job may go down because of re-deploying for a bug fix, etc, you just need to be lucky enough to listen to a not busy topic and then have a downtime when offsets are expired.

Typically in prod-ready environment when all the data matters you wouldn't use `latest`, you would use `earliest` though. This is because often you want to handle all the data you have. If it happens to be that the consumer group is deployed 1st time (or changed) you (re)-process all the data. 
What happens then, if the offsets are lost/expired and the job is restarted, it would trigger full re-processing again, which is probably undesirable.

This is why things like `samza` is re-committing offsets every minute or so. And `kafka-streams` (and I believe any Java consumer) do the same.

I think that allowing jobs to re-assure their offsets would be logical in many reasons.

> Turns out `-O2` changes the behaviour of the shift, while `-O0` gives the
same result. Shifting is defined as *"unsigned divide by 2 X times"* in the
Intel's ASM manual. So why doesn't it return zero? […] Shifting by 33 actually
behaves like shifting by 1, since `shr` is shift *and* rotate:
>
> > *In a logical shift instruction (also referred to as unsigned shift),
the bits that slide off the end disappear (except for the last, which goes
into the carry flag), and the spaces are always filled with zeros.
Logical shifts are best used with unsigned numbers.*
>
> "Always filled with zeroes" doesn't seem to be the case here.
>
> […]
>
> Turns out the algorithm was originally designed for 64 bits, since the
document IDs are longs. For 32 bits however it's a no-op. The developers
didn't even read the algorithm. This code is equivalent:
> ```swift
> func hashIdsOpt(_ ids: [Int32]) -> Int32 {
>     var acc: UInt32 = 0
> 
>     for id in ids {
>         acc = (acc &* 20261)
>         acc = (acc &* 20261) &+ UInt32(bitPattern: id)
>     }
>     return Int32(bitPattern: acc & UInt32(0x7FFFFFFF))
> }
> ```
>
> So there were supposed to be a version for both 64 and 32 bits but they
simply copy-pasted the 64 bit version for 32, which worked surprisingly
only because they used high level language on x86_64 (Swift). Java was fine
too and on iOS they saw no issues because ARM handles shift by integer size
"correctly".

> def hash20261_32(ints):
>     acc = 0

Suppose there is a thread that uses several solvers for various operations. The solvers are used either sequentially or there could even be multiple solvers created, with only one being actually used for solving at each time (in order to have multiple solver stacks). Now we want to interrupt the solver, for example, to implement a time limit per operation, but do so only for specific solver instances (e.g., because some cheap but high-priority operations should always be able to finish, but other operations should be cancelled, or there are different timeouts per operation, or something similar).

Currently this can only be done checking before interrupting which operation the main thread is currently running, but this check is either racy or requires synchronization for all solver accesses. With the possibility to interrupt individual solvers, it would be much easier to implement this.

So the first thing to learn would be: When you do a minor change to an existing set of work, it's better to amend the commit you had. The general way would be:
```
# first, edit the files
git add ...  # then, stage the changes
git commit --amend --no-edit  # now, instead of adding a new commit, amend the existing one
git push --force  # whenever you amend something, you need a force push
```

To merge the last 2 commits into one, do the following:
```
git reset --soft HEAD~1  # go one step back in history, but keep all changes staged
git commit --amend  # this merges the changes into the commit you're pointing at
```

About the relu changes: I think it will be the easiest to redo them now, and then commit them (this time as a separate commit, because that change doesn't have anything to do with the objective functions). I don't know which commit they were originally included in, otherwise you could do `git cherry-pick ...`.

Why I'm so keen on a clean history is that it makes it a lot easier to track down breaking changes later -- even with 100% test coverage, it can happen that some change introduces some bug, and it's a lot easier if changes are grouped into consistent packages (commits) than if they're mixing different things or only doing half of the work. Yes, a leaky rectifier with learnable leakiness is called a Parametric ReLU (PReLU) in literature, and available as [`lasagne.layers.ParametricRectifierLayer`](http://lasagne.readthedocs.io/en/latest/modules/layers/special.html#lasagne.layers.ParametricRectifierLayer). It's not in `lasagne.nonlinearities` because nonlinearities in Lasagne are supposed to be fixed functions, they do not have an API to register trainable parameters. To use the parametric rectifier, just place it after the layer you want the nonlinearity to be applied to, and set that layer's nonlinearity to `None` or `lasagne.nonlinearities.identity` (that's the same):
```python
from lasagne.layers import ParametricRectifierLayer as Prelu
layer = InputLayer(...)
layer = Prelu(DenseLayer(layer, num_units=..., nonlinearity=None))
```

Concurrent live sessions are not supported.
There are caveats around this but to first order concurrent sessions aren't supported.
You get a line at the top warning you in red that another session is running. Do you not see this or are you proposing we make some modifications to this?

This will likely not change in the near future as we make extensive use of an XML repository to store job information on disk. What you're seeing is a result of trying to access the same ```prep``` repository from multiple instances at the same time which isn't supported.

Here, the ```prep``` repository is locked by one Ganga session due to Ganga currently having no safe mechanism for releasing access to the various repositories on disk after actions have finished with it. (This is a long standing issue relating to many issues around both concurrent access and the prep repository being not particularly well understood).
When a second Ganga session attempts use this the same repository the access will fail which is what you're seeing above.

Or,
What you're seeing is that after Ganga has exited uncleanly the session lock files are persisting from one session to another.
This can be fixed by removing the session lockfiles under ```gangadir/repository```. This is automatically done by Ganga once the lockfile is reasonably stale, however Ganga has to run on systems with _very_ slow file access so the timeout on lockfiles is set to be something similar to 30sec, which means that launching Ganga immediately after it crashes causes issues similar to what you see above.


The history management within a 'live' Ganga session is managed solely within the IPython external package. (Currently IPython 1.2.1 for Ganga 6.1.16)
We're looking to upgrade this to IPython 4.x in the near future but at the moment is limited by our dependence on Python 2.6 for our target environments.
As of 6.1.2 I think you're still using a very old version of IPython (0.6.12 if memory serves) and I can't comment on what may or may not be broken in this for different systems.

@milliams asked about the original intention of this code. I think it is/was as follows.
1. Create a list of all sub-jobs that should be monitored.
2. Split this list into different backends.
3. Place calls to the backend specific monitoring on a queue which a thread pool picks
    tasks from **if** previous call has finished and the time since that backend was last queued
   is above the minimal cycle time.
4. Repeat from 1.

So point 3. is obviously the tricky one. 
   * You do not want to risk that you stack up monitoring requests on the queue for a given backend.
   * You do not want to monitor too often if the loop is more or less empty. The minimal cycle time
     I think should be different for different backends (for a Local job I expect to be notified just a few
     seconds after the job has finished while for a Grid/Cloud backend even a few minutes is fine.

@RCMariko Thanks for clarifying. The goal seems to be to streamline the process for running and implementing integration tests, so that the bar for contributions can be set higher. That's a nice goal. However, there are some significant challenges and drawbacks to the proposal as written. I've listed a few below. Perhaps it is possible to come up with some other techniques for streamlining things that avoids the challenges and drawbacks I've identified. I'd love to brainstorm and explore the problem space. Perhaps some git-subdir, git-subtree, git-submodule, or make magic could suffice.

>  I would welcome hearing of negative consequences.

OK. Let me start with a familiar example. Let's say that I have a host on which I've installed Pulp 3 from PyPI or RPM packages, and let's say that I'd like to run integration tests against it. How would I do that? The current approach is as folows:

1. Create a Python 3.4+ virtualenv.
2. Install Pulp Smash. (Either from source or from PyPI.)
3. Configure Pulp Smash. (e.g. `python -m pulp_smash settings create`)
4. Run the tests. (e.g. `python -m unittest discover pulp_smash.tests`)

What happens if this issue is implemented, and integration tests for pulp_ansible are moved into the pulp_ansible repository? **Tests for pulp_ansible won't run.** This is because `pip install pulp-smash` (or whatever equivalent was used at install time) won't pull in the pulp_ansible integration tests.

"Ah, but I can just install both Pulp Smash and pulp_ansible into the same virtualenv, and let the former discover the latter's [namespace packages](https://packaging.python.org/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/)!"

Will that work? Here's some of the issues that need to be overcome:

* Pulp Smash and pulp_ansible must be installed on the same host, and pulp_ansible must be installed into a standard search path. Both of these requirements are problematic:
  * Pulp Smash and pulp_ansible are frequently present on different hosts. This is the case on our current Jenkins testing infrastructure, but it's only by coincidence. The push to drop nodepool from Jenkins might invalidate this assumption. Implementation of multi-host testing would certainly invalidate this assumption. This assumption is already invalidated for me, as I have one Pulp Smash installation for many Pulp VMs.
  * The Pulp 3 installer (both from-source and from-PyPI) install Pulp and its plugins into non-standard paths.
* Pulp Smash would need to be re-designed to support namespace packages. One of the big problems with namespace packages is namespace conflicts, where a name in a newly imported package conflicts with a name in the base application or one of the other namespace packages.
* Pulp Smash has no stable API. Code is periodically moved around or changed internally, sometimes in major ways. If third parties were to integrate with Pulp Smash, a stable API would need to be implemented. At the very least, this implies paying off some technical debt (e.g. moving more stuff out of `pulp_smash.*` into `pulp_smash.tests.pulp2.*`). And implementing a stable API means Pulp Smash can't innovate and improve like it can now. 
* Splitting code across repositories means that the linters and unit tests built in to Pulp Smash won't be able to work their magic on the separated code. (It may still be doable with some technical magic.)
* Pulp Smash is a non-trivial application. It's reasonable to think that its requirements and pulp_ansible's requirements might conflict, meaning that installing them into the same virtualenv won't work.

Hi @danpalmer,

I think your concerns regarding the "pythonic" way of doing things are not just valid, but very reasoned and thoughtful.

Before adding into the docs, I would love to describe here the "whys" on each of the points you made, so we can go back and forth with the explanations until they are clear. And from there we can put them into the docs.

## Promises

> What is a promise? Promises aren't a concept in Python, and it might be assumed that async/await could be used instead. Providing a brief reason for why promises are used in Graphene and links to further documentation would be great.

You are completely right, with `async`/`await` syntax the promise abstraction is no longer needed (I even tweeted about it [few months ago](https://twitter.com/syrusakbary/status/874001608115232768) 😜).

The main reason Graphene/GraphQL-core was using `Promise`s in the first place is for the need of a abstraction "future" to be managed in Python versions where the `async`/`await` syntax was not available (Python 2.7+, Python ~< 3.4).

It's also true that in Python and some of its libs the concept of `Deferred` and `Future` was also available, but there were some reasons for using a `Promise` abstraction instead:
1) It provides a "universal" way of working with futures/deferrals independently of the framework (gevent, tornado, threading, ...., as each of this implementation have a different implementation of a `Future`).
2) It provides a very easy API to chain this "future" values.
3) It incredibly simplified the GraphQL core codebase, making it easy for contributors to step on to the code: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/pull/

But the truth is, once Python 3 is used by most of the Graphene users, the promise abstraction can be safely removed :)

## `self` in resolvers
> self not being a class instance in resolver 'methods'. I don't understand the architectural reasons why this can't be the case, but in Python an instance method (like the resolver methods) is expected to take a self first argument. The documentation says that this isn't the case with Graphene (which is unexpected), but it still calls the argument self in some cases which further confuses the issue. It is mentioned that these methods act like static methods, however it's unexpected that they aren't decorated with the @staticmethod decorator.

At the beginning (in Graphene), all resolvers were receiving the args `self, args, info`, where `self` was actually an instance of the `ObjectType` itself.
So, for accessing the `root` value, the developer need to type: `self.root`. Also, for ease of development, a `__getattr__` was created in the `ObjectType` so each time you do `self.abc` it was calling `getattr(self.root, 'abc')`.

This resulted in very bad performance in the earlier versions of Graphene (previous to `1.0`), as for each `ObjectType` resolution, the result needed to be wrapped in a new instance of the `ObjectType`.

So then, we have to solve other question also... why all resolvers are static without the need of them being decorated with `@staticmethod`?
The main reason for that is:
1) To simplify the code necessary to be written by the developer
2) If the developer forgets to wrap it with `@staticmethod`, there will be no real `self` (as we don't want to wrap the result because of performance as exposed before).

A very similar decision was made by the Python committee when the new `__init_subclass__` method was introduced in [PEP-487](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0487/#requiring-an-explicit-decorator-on-init-subclass) choosing not to require a `@classmehtod` on the method, mainly because of minifying developer issues.

Eventually, both the Graphene types and the real datatypes will be merged into the same object (for static typing reasons, plus readability, I can explain this in another comment if necessary), so this hopefully will no longer be an issue.

## Interfaces not inherited

> Interfaces are listed in a Meta class attribute. In normal Python I would expect these to be in the inheritance tree, perhaps as an abstract base class using abc. Again I'm sure there are great reasons why this is not the case, but giving some motivation for this in the documentation would go a long way to making Graphene easier to understand.

In the first versions of Graphene (pre `1.0`), the only way to inherit a specific `Interface` in a `ObjectType` was through Python object inheritance (rather than meta like right now, see the [Graphene 1.0 upgrade guide](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/UPGRADE-v1.0.md))
However, the nature of `ObjectTypes` and `Interfaces` are mainly different (both hold fields, but one can be instantiated while the other don't...), mixing both classes to be inherited at the same time can result challenging:
1) When a class inherits an `Interface`, how can we check that is an `ObjectType` versus an abstract `Interface`? (like the Node interface).
2) How we can make both metaclasses not to collide (`ObjectType` metaclass and `Interface` metaclass).

Right now I believe this might be easier resolved thanks to the `__init_subclass__` approach, but will wait until a valid (and easy to understand) proof of concept is made.

## Middleware as a class rather than a function
> Middleware is implemented object instances with a resolve method. Again I'm sure there are reasons for this, but I wonder if the class is needed, could middleware just be a function (I think this would be more Pythonic). Alternatively, middleware could be specified to Graphene as classes, not instances, and Graphene could instantiate on each request-response cycle so that the instance is able to hold data in the request lifecycle. This is how Django does it, and it can be useful with more complex middleware. Right now that is not well supported.

The reason Middleware was created as a class was because of it was created with the aim, of not only act in the resolution of a field, but also intercepting the beginning/end of a `GraphQL` query.
But the truth, is this was planned, but never added afterwards.

About middleware as just a function, it's also supported by Graphene, but sadly not documented.

-------------------------

Would love to hear your thoughts on this! :)

I agree that this could probably use a little clarification in the docs, but if you look at it as pure Python:
1- graphene.String is a class, you use the class when you want to say I want a List of Strings. List(String) means you want a list of object of type String. The argument to List(Structure).__init__ is actually called 'of_type': https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/graphene/types/structures.py#L10

2- graphene.String() is an instance of the UnmountedType String just like List() is an instance of the type List.

If wouldn't make sense to say you want a list of a particular instance of the String class. Inversely, when you're using String to create a Field, you do want an instance in that case. I don't want a field to equal the String class, I want an instance of the type, an actual String instance.

I hope this clarifies it a bit.

The existing syntax pointed out by @jagerman is simple enough. I don't think that `array_t(1, 2, 3)` adds much over the existing `array_t({1, 2, 3})`. The latter has the advantage that it can naturally be extended with more arguments (strides, data) while keeping the same form, which is also consistent with the numpy array creation syntax. 

While possible to implement, the braceless syntax requires adding a completely generic variadic constructor with SFINAE alongside a bunch of existing constructors. This would surely cause more trouble down the line (more SFINAE for other constructors if they are to be made more generic, which was proposed elsewhere).

It might also be nice to drop the "_48" for the default size (i.e. for the size used for a typical `unique_ptr` holder).  So then we'd have `pybind11_builtins.object` typically, but the size suffix (e.g. `pybind11_builtins.object_56`) for other types (e.g. a type with a `shared_ptr` holder).

I kind of don't like that data is combined with data_at in your implementation but maybe it's just to foreign to experience.  Definitely don't like the data vs mutable_data variant, but maybe that's the best option...  I still figure users will often be using data() + some_offset often enough... maybe bytes is the answer, though I'd still prefer p() shorthand considering how many times I think I'm likely to type it.

As much as I like metaprogramming and functional programming, size() implementation would probably be best to forloop out, same with get_byte_offset.  Not just for ensuring good speed for such frequently used routines but that code will be explosive/distracting/high penalty in debug mode.  How often do people debug indexes? :-)

Overall comments - given the nature of writing libraries in C/C++ and calling from python not just for wrapping, but for speed, we need to offer no-runtime check paths or disable them when NDEBUG is defined, but there are cases where we want checks on still too.  Maybe that comes from operator() vs .at, maybe _nothrow.  Recurring theme: preserving ndarray's generality and existing behaviors - but do allow things to go faster.

Noting index_at implementation is faulty and assumes packed arrays, should just use dims and itemsize - for loop only again.

numpy .astype returns a copy, .view lets you change the dtype without copy and where some checks on itemsize are performed.  Follow numpy semantics for principal of least surprise.

char as *ptr type for Array-Proxy, not sure if this should be char - AFAICR I don't think C++11 specified char to be 1 byte, but uint8_t is guaranteed to and in C++11.

Don't fall into the trap of everywhere handling packed data, the assumption insidiously creeps abound.  Maybe you have an optimized specialization again (through flags or type) that has that feature and overloads indexing methods, but in general, something like array_t<T>::in, and ::out should not assume packed.  Make those optimizations opt-in.

Thanks for the PR!

I see this as an important addition to the existing functionality of DNS plugins, and we definitely will need to handle it.

I read through the PR code, and there's a few points I would like to raise for discussion:
 
 - I can see how this could cause confusion in user, and/or plugin behavior, as the CNAME can and often will point to a different DNS server and/or API. eg. using Route 53 plugin as the zone root exists in Route 53, but the CNAME points to a Cloudflare instance. Or to a same API, but with different credentials.
 - This is functionality that's tailored towards more advanced configuration, and adding complexity in form of CNAME chain resolution seems like something that we could omit here. The user should know where the CNAME points to, and what to update.
 - The PR adds a new dependency (dnspython), and we're trying to trim down dependencies as much as possible, as they cause a lot of extra work for distribution package maintainers.

That said, I really would love to see the functionality in our range of DNS plugins as it is an important and effective way to avoid storing credentials to the actual DNS zone in the boxes themselves.

I would propose following changes to the PR:
 
 - Removing the autodetection code (CNAME chain resolving), and hence avoid adding the new dependency.
 - Making the new CLI flag something along the lines of: 
`--dns-force-record=where.cname.points.to.tld`, which would simply be the actual record that the plugin needs to update instead of the one derived from `-d yourdomain.tld`

These changes would make it clear from the get go which plugin, and which credentials need to be used in addition of avoiding adding complexity and a new dependency. 

Let me know what you think!

> One of the issues this creates is there is a function in ToolBase called has_role that depends on the existence of the roles launch param which no longer exists in LTI 2.

Luckily, this isn't correct. https://www.imsglobal.org/specs/ltiv2p0/implementation-guide#toc-39

LTI 2 roles do make things more complicated, though, because they define a different way to specify the same roles, and some of the new roles are ambiguous for attempting to convert to the old roles. It's annoying. The `params` branch may be instructional for learning a bit more about what I mean.

> In LTI2 many Launch Params are gone, including:
context_id, context_title, lis_*, resource_link_title, roles, user_id

I'll note that LTI doesn't say that parameters not specified are not allowed to be present. It merely doesn't define what they mean. And of course it doesn't promise that there won't be a conflict in the future, where if you use `custom_` or `ext_` namespaced parameters it promises to not conflict.

In my opinion, it's the sanest approach to allow all launch parameters, specified by any version of LTI, for all LTI versions. Technically we could allow arbitrary parameters, but I don't know that that would actually be helpful, since it would mask more subtle errors, such as parameter name misspellings.

What happens is that the Dataset constructor doesn't know how to sort the `examples` in batches since your dataset can have arbitrarily named fields (in this case, `name` and `label`). I guess you want to sort over the length of `name`, but the dataset can't know that. Maybe you want to sort examples in the batch over `label`.

So - a fix (and I guess intended usage) would be to manually set the `sort_key` in iterator constructor. The `Iterator` only backs off to the `Dataset.sort_key` if you don't pass the `sort_key` argument to the iterator. An example:

```python
sort_key = lambda ex: len(ex.name)
# ...
train_iter, test_iter = data.BucketIterator.splits((train_dataset, test_dataset), batch_size=32,  device=-1, repeat=False, sort_key=sort_key)
```
This is done to minimize the padding within batches. If you don't want to do this, this is the alternative:

```python
train_iter, test_iter = data.BucketIterator.splits((train_dataset, test_dataset), batch_size=32,  device=-1, repeat=False, sort=False, sort_within_batch=False)

```
